titleStmt.title: Morocco – Zaer – Ain Aouda – Women (2024)
WIBARAB Corpus
titleStmt.funder: Funded by the European Research Council under the Grant Agreement 101020127

publicationStmt.distributor: Austrian Center for Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage

recording.p: Recorded during Morocco Fieldwork Campaign Terlan Djavadova 2022

prefixDef.p: Private URIs using the teiCorpusHeader prefix are pointers to any element in the WIBARAB teiCorpus document.
Private URIs using the share prefix are pointers to audio file residing on the WIBARAB network share.
Private URIs using the geo prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a place element in VICAV Geodata list.
Private URIs using the dmp prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB DMP document..
Private URIs using the zotid prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a biblStruct element in the TEI export of the VICAV Zotero Group library.
Private URIs using the sources prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB list of sources.

spanGrp.span: All of this is mine, I am born and raised here, the father and the mother, all born here in Ain Aouda, the ancestors were from Ain Aouda.
We were living according to the circumstances. We didn't have money, we were poor. My mum was grinding with the handmill, there was no grinder. She was grinding flour.
There was only...only with firewood. We make it like this. We were grinding and flour comes out so we can make bread.
We had oven out of clay, out of soil, we were putting soil and we were putting firewood.

We put the firewood on this side and the bread on this side. and it bakes and we make this ... we make something callled batbut, msemmen. We make couscous, there was no couscous from the store. we made it with our hands and cooked it.
we were ... [...] there was no ... we were making only butter and milk ... and we ate this, so that we could live.
We were ... [...] we were living in the nature a lot [...] the coutryside, we were going out, only the countryside.
We were living only on the countryside. We didn't have light, this, we didn't have. We had candles.
We didn't had gas, now there are gas bottles. We were cooking on the firewood.
We were cooking in a tajine out of clay.
There was a ... they call it hawwar, also he is out of clay,that's what we used for cooking, only with firewood. We didn't had a gas barrel, there was no ...
We were going by feet, we were collecting wood on our backs, firewood.
We brought them, to burn them, to eat, we didn't had clothes.
We didn't had clothes. They were giving us clothes only with a voucher, fabric only with a voucher and we were making (clothes).
We were sewing on our own so that we could wear it.
We didn't had trousers, this.
We were walking only by foot, when we wanted to go to Rabat, we went - with all due respect - with a moule or a donkey.
How a guest is coming? Whatever ... we found, which he found, he eats with us, he eats with us ... he can stay over night, he can go, depending on the possibility.
We slaughter cattle for him and they are happy because of him, who don't have cattles, slaughters a chicken and they eat it for dinner and he stays over night with them and in the morning he goes.

No, now everything is good, now everytihng is from the shop, everything is available [...] so, we have everything, if you want to go to the shop to buy, it's not like in the past, in the past we didnt find the light, we could make a fire only ... with which we made light.

The wedding, when one wanted to marry, take a girl, he didn't see her, you didn't know how he was [...] That's it, they take her ... you didn't see, he didn't see you, they gave you, they took you, they married me off, I didn't know the man ... the man, when I met him, he was 62 years and I, I was 16. I didn't know him, he didn't know me and he had many children, he had children, and they gave me to, because of ... to the old man. yeah, I gave birth with him to children (as well).

yeah, that's how they take you, leave ... they give you, you didn't know (him) till they make the wedding, they take youto the man, whoever he was. [Now it is the same or not?] No, now it became, no... the man sees the girl and the girl sees the man ... not like before. In the past they just took you, they covered your eyes, from 12 years or 13 years they gave ... that's it, you accept, no, no, you have to accept, from this, you only say when you say "I dont want." or ... they give you and you are obliged.
And that's it, they make a wedding ... you go, you still find the man, also he ... you keep milking the cows ... you keep milking the cows ...

They come to engage you and the woman goes, two women go, they come to you and she says to you: "We want that girl." and the father says, he says to her: "Okay, I agree with you, I will give her to you." That's it, they come to engage you, one makes like that, they come ... [... ] they bring you clothes, ???, they bring you Henna, they put henna on you [...] like this, they put henna on you, bring you clothes, clothes, they bring you henna, they put henna on you, completely, and they go again, some days pass, they come either to take you on thursday or they take you on saturday, they took you to the house of the groom.

They make ... they come, they bring cattle and they slaughter it, one tells you: "His zgharit" and they take you, you go to the house of the groom ... dancing, they dance and they give, you go saying goodbye to the mother, the fahter, they say goodbye to you, and than you go to your house ... the day after you go again, the man makes a wedding for you and dances and singing ... and that's it, you go like this, that's what he does for her.

Zaer is famous for barbeque [I heard of it, from which animal?] sheep, they grill it and they make couscous, they make weddings and barbeques for it, they make couscous with meat of beef and vegetables. This is what Zaer is famous for. [...] rfisa with butter, this they cook it, they make it with harsha.

And they decorate it with eggs of organic chicken, rfisa, they decorate it with eggs and they... butter, milk [...] semolina, with semolina, we make harsha and we make it with butter, with milk and we press it flat and we put in milk and we put butter in it and we put eggs boiled in water and we decorate it with it.

In the past, when we were cooking with fire, on the coals, we made a tea pot [...] water was cooked [...] you, translate for me! We make water cooking in the teapot, with firewood and we make tea in a pot [...] we make tea, we rinse it [...]
We put it in the tea pot, yeah, we put tea and we rinse it, we pour water and than again we rinse it and ... we put sugar and put it (on the fire) so it boils.

We make tea with Nanamint, we make tea with verbena, we make tea with wormwood, there is oregano, there is also mint, there is majoram, there is ... pennyrile, there is wild mint, there is ... this other kind of mint ... [what is marsita?] its like nanamint.
This is what we make, we make ... we call it ... herbal, so herbal tea.
We had one (of them) one calls it ferrah and hawwar and ... if they want to talk to you, they say: way!" [What is way?] That means, like, what are you saying ... [...] spoon, they call it also spoon.

We were making, it's called, to make lben, we were making shekwa... we make it out of sheep or out of a goat and we skiff it off and we tan it with a tanner of ... sumach, it becomes red and they keep shaking it strongly with lben inside.
So for washing (we used), we call it bstiliya [...] it is a washbasin, we don't say washbasin, we say bastiliya.
